people.bio:
Samuel Bourne (30 October 1834 – 1912) was one of the most celebrated travel photographers of the nineteenth century. Born in Munslow, Shropshire, he became a bank clerk in Nottingham before turning to photography. Between 1863 and 1870 he made three extended photographic expeditions to India, producing thousands of images of the subcontinent including the Himalayas, Kashmir, and major Indian cities. He was a partner in the firm Bourne & Shepherd, one of the most important commercial photography studios in colonial India, based in Calcutta and Simla. Returning to England around 1870, he continued in business as a manufacturer. He lived at Adam House (previously known as Brightlands) on Clumber Road East in the Park Estate.

Bourne left Moore and Robinson's to become a professional photographer in India. Based in Simla at the "Bourne and Shepherd" studio, Bourne travelled widely in India from 1863 to 1870 before returning to England in 1871 with approximately 2,200 images of the landscape and architecture of India and the Himalayas. These photographs established Bourne's reputation as the greatest photographer of Britain's colonies and one of the finest travel photographers of the nineteenth century.

In 1867 he married Mary Tolley, daughter of a wealthy Nottingham businessman.

In 1877 he became a cotton doubler in partnership with his brother-in-law James B. Tolley. They built a cotton doubling works in Netherfield in 1881. Bourne became a magistrate and was also President of the Nottingham Tennis Club.

Bourne commissioned Arthur Marshall to construct Brightlands in 1885. Bourne lived at Brightlands from its construction until his death in 1912.

Samuel and Mary had 5 children: Constance Edith Bourne (b. c.1870–1958), Florence Gertrude Bourne (b. 1873), Lina Mary Bourne (b. 1874), Stanley Bourne (1876–1942), and Eveline Annie Bourne (b. 1879–1965).

On 29th March 1949 the lease on the property was assigned from the Exors. S. Bourne to S. & F.G. Blythen. The freehold was purchased by Blythen in 1953 for £2,000.

occupations.notes: Bourne & Shepherd, Calcutta and Simla; major expeditions to India and the Himalayas 1863–1870

people.bio: Stanley Bourne (1876–1942) married Eileen Mary Michael in 1911. They had 4 children: Roland Michael, Humphrey Samuel, Edward J. Stanley, and Angela Mary Bourne. Stanley continued in the textile industry, moving from cotton to rayon, and became a millionaire and (by 1937) Deputy Chairman of Courtaulds Ltd. It seems that Stanley Bourne continued to hold the lease on Brightlands until his death in 1942.
Constance Edith Bourne (b. c.1870–1958) died unmarried in Nottingham in 1958.
Florence Gertrude Bourne (b. 1873) married Alfred C. Pearse (American?) in 1913 and died in 1914.

Lina Mary Bourne (b. 1874) married Hubert Arthur Dowson (b. 1866) in Nottingham in 1900. Hubert Arthur was the son of Benjamin Dowson of Felixstowe, Clumber Road West in 1891 & 1901. In 1901 Lina and Hubert were living on Western Terrace. The Dowsons lived at Woodborough Hall from 1923 to 1937. Hubert was a solicitor in Nottingham — manager (1919), trustee (1932) and Chairman (1937) of the Nottingham Trustee Savings Bank. The partnership of Dowson, Enfield and Co. of 13 Weekday Cross was dissolved on 20th May 1940. Hubert was knighted in 1937, at which time he was President of the Law Society. Hubert played for Nottingham Rugby Football Club. Hubert died in 1946. Their children included Benjamin Hubert, Enid Mary and Maud Susanna Stanley.

Eveline Annie Bourne (b. 1879–1965) married the architect Edmund Herbert Child (b. Halifax, c.1866; d. 1938) in 1906. They were living at 24 The Ropewalk in 1911. From 1900–04 Child was a partner in Child, Gorman and Ross which had offices in Long Eaton, Nottingham and London.

significant_places.name: Bourne & Shepherd, Calcutta
significant_places.description: Bourne & Shepherd was one of the most important commercial photography studios in colonial India, with premises in Calcutta and Simla. Samuel Bourne of Adam House was a founding partner. The studio produced thousands of images of the Indian subcontinent, many of the highest technical quality of the era.

person_connections.connection: Partner in Bourne & Shepherd; made three major photographic expeditions to India 1863–1870

significant_places.name: Courtaulds
significant_places.description: Courtaulds was one of Britain's largest textile manufacturers. Stanley Bourne, son of Samuel Bourne of Adam House, served as Deputy Chairman of the company.
